scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Yesterday in ED majors I assessed a 62-year-old man with pleuritic chest pain. I considered ACS, PE and pneumonia, used Wells score, reviewed ECG and CXR, discussed with my consultant, and arranged CTPA. I learned to state my risk assessment more clearly when escalating.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a 62-year-old with pleuritic chest pain, formulated a differential diagnosis, reviewed investigations, and discussed the clinical reasoning/management plan with a consultant.
The trainee includes a specific reflection on their communication and escalation style ('learned to state my risk assessment more clearly when escalating').

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I assessed a 62-year-old man presenting with pleuritic chest pain in ED majors. My differential diagnosis included acute coronary syndrome (ACS), pulmonary embolism (PE), and pneumonia.

To risk-stratify the patient, I calculated a Wells score, reviewed the ECG, and evaluated the chest X-ray. I then discussed the clinical presentation and my findings with the ED consultant to agree on the diagnostic plan, which led to arranging a CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA).
draft_fields.reflection: This case of structured escalation. I learned that I need to state my risk assessment and clinical scoring more clearly and explicitly when discussing cases with senior colleagues to a shared understanding of patient risk and to streamline decision-making.
key_capabilities: SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Today I performed DC cardioversion for unstable atrial fibrillation as a formal DOPS. My consultant observed each step directly. I checked consent, monitoring and sedation plan, delivered a synchronised shock, and reflected on calling for airway support earlier.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Direct Observation of Procedural Skills.

draft_fields.trainee_performance: I performed a direct observation of procedural skills (DOPS) for a synchronised DC cardioversion under the direct observation of my consultant. I verified the patient's consent, ensured all monitoring was in place, and prepared the sedation plan. I then safely delivered the synchronised shock.
draft_fields.reflection: On reflection, while the procedure was completed successfully, I should have called for airway support earlier in the process to optimal safety during the sedation phase.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC2: be expert in fluid management and circulatory support in critically ill patients (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.form_name: Direct Observation of Procedural Skills - ACCS
scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. During my ACCS anaesthetic placement I inserted a chest drain using Seldinger technique as an observed DOPS on 5 July 2026. My supervisor watched the procedure. I confirmed landmarks, used sterile technique and reflected on slowing down before dilating.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Direct Observation of Procedural Skills.

draft_fields.case_observed: I inserted a chest drain using the Seldinger technique under direct supervision. I confirmed the anatomical landmarks, maintained a strict sterile technique, and performed each step of the procedure sequentially.
draft_fields.reflection: I need to I slow down and double-check my depth and positioning before using the dilator. Taking a deliberate pause at this stage helps prevent displacement or damage to surrounding structures.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

issues: Target form DOPS_ACCS not in recommendations: DOPS

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. A consultant directly observed me taking a focused history and examination from a 7-year-old with wheeze in paediatric ED. I explained the plan to the parent and received feedback on structuring my assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee was directly observed by a consultant while performing a focused history, clinical examination, and communicating the management plan to the parent in real time.
The trainee received feedback on structuring their paediatric assessment, making a reflection log appropriate to document their learning and how they will adjust their future practice.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning: I took a focused history and performed a clinical examination on a 7-year-old presenting with a wheeze in the paediatric ED.
draft_fields.reflection: The consultant directly observed my assessment and provided feedback on structuring my history and physical examination in a paediatric setting, as well as how I communicated the management plan to the parent. I will focus on implementing this structured approach to my clinical assessments are systematic and my communication with parents remains clear and reassuring during busy shifts.
key_capabilities: SLO5 KC1: be expert in assessing and managing all children and young adult patients attending the ED (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. On an evening acute take I managed six patients across majors and resus, including sepsis, chest pain and a frail fall. My consultant observed my prioritisation, handover and escalation decisions over the shift.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a cohort of six acute patients across majors and resuscitation, with a consultant observing their clinical decision-making, prioritisation, and management over the shift.
The consultant observed the trainee's non-technical skills (prioritisation, handover, and escalation decisions) across multiple cases during a substantial shift period.

draft_fields.cases_observed: During an evening acute take, I managed six patients across majors and resuscitation. The cases included presentations of sepsis, chest pain, and a frail patient following a fall.
draft_fields.reflection:
This shift required active prioritisation and dynamic decision-making to safely manage multiple high-acuity patients simultaneously. I focused on maintaining a clear overview of the department, timely escalation of deteriorating patients and structured handovers to the oncoming team.

My consultant observed my approach to managing these cases, including how I prioritised clinical tasks and coordinated care. Discussing my escalation decisions helped reinforce the importance of structured communication during high-pressure shifts to patient safety is maintained.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I acted as EPIC for a crowded evening ED shift. I allocated junior doctors, held a safety huddle, escalated capacity concerns to site management and reflected on protecting time for re-review of high-risk patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee acted in the EPIC (Emergency Physician in Charge) leadership role during a crowded shift, coordinating juniors, holding a safety huddle, and managing departmental capacity.
The trainee reflects on the operational challenges of the shift and identifies a specific area for future practice: protecting time for the re-review of high-risk patients.

draft_fields.leadership_context: I was acting as the Doctor-in-Charge (EPIC) during a crowded evening shift in the Emergency Department. The environment was highly pressurised due to department crowding and high patient volume, requiring active coordination of the junior medical team and close liaison with hospital site management.
draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I took on the role of EPIC to manage the flow and clinical safety of the department during a busy evening shift. My actions included allocating junior doctors to appropriate clinical areas based on department demand and their individual competencies. 

I held a multidisciplinary safety huddle to the team was aligned on high-risk patients and departmental bottlenecks. To address the capacity pressures, I escalated our status and specific crowding concerns directly to the hospital site management team to coordinate wider trust support.
draft_fields.reflection:
Managing a crowded shift as EPIC highlighted the difficulty of balancing operational flow with clinical oversight. It is easy to become consumed by bed management and escalation processes, which can distract from the clinical supervision of the junior team.

I realised the importance of actively protecting time to re-review high-risk patients and support juniors with complex decision-making. On future leadership shifts, I will establish a more structured routine to step away from the flow desk regularly to conduct clinical touchpoints with the junior staff.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC2: will be able to liaise with the rest of the acute/urgent care team and wider hospital as shift leader (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE. I framed a PICO question, searched NICE and a recent meta-analysis, and discussed how I would apply the evidence safely in low-risk patients. I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee formulated a clinical question (PICO), searched literature (NICE, meta-analysis), and appraised evidence on age-adjusted D-dimer.
The trainee includes clear reflective elements, specifically noting what they learned about documenting the limits of evidence when communicating risk.

draft_fields.situation: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE.
draft_fields.pico_outcome: Safe diagnostic exclusion of pulmonary embolism
draft_fields.search_methodology: I searched NICE guidelines and a recent meta-analysis regarding age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE.
draft_fields.evidence_evaluation: I appraised the evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE from NICE and a recent meta-analysis.
draft_fields.apply_to_practice: I discussed how I would apply the evidence safely in low-risk patients.
draft_fields.communicate_to_patient: I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.
draft_fields.reflection: This appraisal helped me understand how to apply age-adjusted D-dimer thresholds safely in low-risk patients. I realized the importance of clearly documenting the limits of the evidence when communicating diagnostic risk to patients.
key_capabilities: SLO10 KC1: be able to appraise, synthesise, communicate and use research evidence to develop EM care (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

A consultant observed the session and gave feedback on pacing and checking understanding.

recommended_forms.rationale: Formal teaching session with observation/assessment stated explicitly.

SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I reviewed feedback from nurses, juniors and consultants. Themes were calm leadership and clear explanations, with a development point around documenting senior discussions more consistently.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee has gathered and is reviewing multi-source feedback from a range of colleagues (nurses, juniors, and consultants) regarding their clinical practice, leadership, and communication.

The trainee is actively reflecting on feedback themes (calm leadership, clear explanations) and identifying a specific professional development need regarding the consistency of documenting senior discussions.

draft_fields.context: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. This entry reflects on my Multi-Source Feedback (MSF) containing feedback from nursing staff, junior colleagues, and consultants.
draft_fields.reflection:
I reviewed my MSF feedback, which highlighted strengths in calm leadership and providing clear explanations to both patients and the team.

The main development point identified was the need to document senior discussions more consistently in the patient notes. I will focus on all consultant and senior inputs are explicitly recorded in my future shifts to improve safety and maintain a clear audit trail.

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. Baseline audit was 42 cases, we introduced a triage prompt and re-audit showed improvement from 58 to 39 minutes. I presented it at governance. My PDP summary this year is to improve quality improvement leadership. Next year I will focus on embedding the prompt and supporting re-audit.

recommended_forms.rationale: QI/audit project with measurement and change; QIAT is the specific assessment form.

draft_fields.qi_engagement: I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. I presented the project at our departmental governance meeting.
draft_fields.qi_understanding: This developed my practical understanding of using baseline measurement, an intervention and re-audit to assess change in an ED quality improvement project.
draft_fields.reflection:
We conducted a baseline audit of 42 cases of adult sepsis to evaluate our time to antibiotics. Following this, we introduced a triage prompt as an intervention to flag these patients earlier. The subsequent re-audit demonstrated an improvement in the mean time to antibiotics, reducing from 58 minutes to 39 minutes.

Presenting these findings at the departmental governance meeting allowed me to share the data with the wider team and discuss the clinical impact. This project demonstrated how a simple, targeted systemic prompt can successfully drive clinical improvement and reduce delays in critical treatments.
draft_fields.next_pdp: Next year I will focus on embedding the triage prompt into daily practice and supporting a further re-audit to sustainability.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC1: be able to provide clinical leadership on effective Quality Improvement work (2025 Update)
SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I taught two F1 doctors and a medical student a structured approach to ECG interpretation using five anonymised ECGs from the teaching file. I collected verbal feedback afterwards.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee delivered informal teaching to junior colleagues and a medical student using teaching file ECGs, collecting verbal feedback afterwards without formal assessor observation.
To reflect on the teaching experience, the feedback received from the learners, and how to improve their teaching delivery and facilitation skills in the future.

draft_fields.learning_outcomes: How to apply a structured, systematic approach to ECG interpretation using anonymised clinical cases.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I performed a shoulder reduction in ED with senior advice available but no formal DOPS. I documented neurovascular status before and after and reflected on analgesia planning.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Direct Observation of Procedural Skills.

draft_fields.reflective_comments:
I performed a shoulder reduction in the emergency department yesterday. I senior advice was available if required, though I undertook the procedure independently without a formal DOPS assessment.

I made sure to document the patient's neurovascular status both before and after the manipulation to no compromise occurred during the presentation or the reduction itself. I also reflected on the analgesia planning to the patient was comfortable throughout the process.

In future, I will continue to focus on systematic pre- and post-procedure neurovascular checks and refine my approach to choosing the most effective, patient-tailored analgesia and sedation options for joint reductions.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

issues: Target form PROC_LOG not in recommendations: DOPS

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. On ACCS I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision. I checked contraindications, used sterile technique, obtained CSF samples and reflected on patient positioning.

recommended_forms.rationale: ACCS procedural skill without a clear direct-assessment signal; use the ACCS procedural log.

draft_fields.brief_description: I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision. Prior to the procedure, I systematically checked for contraindications and ensured a sterile technique was maintained throughout. I successfully obtained the required CSF samples for analysis.
draft_fields.reflective_comments: The procedure went well, but it made me reflect on the critical role of patient positioning. Correct positioning is the most vital step in achieving successful dural puncture. The patient is fully flexed and their spine is aligned before starting makes the landmarks much easier to identify and increases the likelihood of a first-pass success.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways, then applied the learning by reviewing our local traffic-light guidance and changing my safety-netting.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee completed an independent self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways to update their clinical knowledge.
The trainee reflects on applying this self-directed learning to their local traffic-light clinical guidance and actively modifying their safety-netting practice.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Self-directed learning: paediatric fever pathways and safety-netting
draft_fields.resource_details: RCEMlearning module on paediatric fever pathways, alongside a review of local traffic-light clinical guidance.
draft_fields.reflection:
I completed a self-directed learning module yesterday on paediatric fever pathways to refresh my clinical knowledge. Following this, I reviewed our local traffic-light guidance to my clinical decision-making aligns with local standards.

This review made me reflect on how I structure my safety-netting instructions for parents. I realized I could make my advice more specific and structured by aligning it directly with the red and amber criteria from the traffic-light system.

I have changed my practice to incorporate these specific criteria into my verbal and written safety-netting plans. This parents have clear, objective indicators for when they need to represent to the department.
key_capabilities: SLO5 KC6: be able to assess and formulate a management plan for children and young adults who present with complex medical and social needs (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

I identified bilateral B-lines, discussed images with my consultant and reflected on integrating ultrasound with clinical assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: Point-of-care ultrasound case is stated explicitly.

draft_fields.clinical_scenario: I assessed a breathless patient with suspected pulmonary oedema.
draft_fields.how_used: I performed a lung ultrasound to evaluate for signs of pulmonary congestion.
draft_fields.usable_images: I was able to obtain usable images and identified bilateral B-lines.
draft_fields.interpret_images: I identified bilateral B-lines, which I then discussed with my consultant to confirm the findings.
draft_fields.learning_points: I reflected on the value of integrating ultrasound findings directly with my clinical assessment to guide the management of acute breathlessness.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I reflected on a complaint about delayed communication with a family during a busy resus shift. The clinical care was appropriate, but I should have allocated a clearer update role and documented conversations better.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee is directly reflecting on a formal or informal patient/family complaint regarding communication delays, focusing on personal learning and future practice adjustments.
The entry contains strong reflective elements regarding communication strategy, role allocation in resuscitation, and documentation practices following a clinical event.

As an ST5 trainee, if this complaint is being analysed from a departmental or systems perspective (e.g., reviewing communication pathways, drafting the formal response, or identifying service improvements), it can fulfill a Management Portfolio assignment.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflection on communication delay during busy resuscitation shift
draft_fields.key_features: A complaint was received regarding a delay in communicating clinical updates to a patient's family during a highly active shift in the resuscitation room.
draft_fields.key_aspects: I provided appropriate clinical care to the patient during their resuscitation. However, due to the high volume of critical patients and the pressure of the shift, I did not establish a dedicated communication plan with the family, nor did I delegate a specific team member to keep them updated. Additionally, my documentation of the brief conversations we did have with the family was insufficient.
draft_fields.learning_points: While clinical management is the immediate priority in a resuscitation scenario, maintaining clear and timely communication with relatives is a core component of patient-centred care. I learned that I must proactively assign a team member to act as a family liaison during high-pressure situations rather than assuming updates will happen organically. I also need to that all communication with families is clearly documented in the medical notes, including the timing and content of the discussion.
draft_fields.further_action: I will actively practice delegating communication roles during resuscitation scenarios, a specific nurse or junior doctor is tasked with updating relatives. I will also that I document any discussions with family members promptly in the patient record.
key_capabilities: SLO7 KC1: have expert communication skills to negotiate, manage complicated or evolving interactions (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC6: demonstrate a positive impact on the culture of the Emergency Department through attitudes and behaviours that impact positively on colleagues, patients and their relatives (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I contributed to a serious incident review after delayed recognition of sepsis in majors. I reviewed the timeline, identified handover gaps and learned to use an explicit trigger for senior review.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee actively contributed to a formal serious incident review regarding delayed sepsis recognition and is reflecting on the clinical and system-level learning.
The trainee is reflecting on personal learning, identifying cognitive/handover gaps, and developing an explicit clinical trigger for senior review to modify future practice.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Serious Incident Review: Delayed Recognition of Sepsis
draft_fields.description:
I contributed to a serious incident review following the delayed recognition of sepsis in a patient in the majors area of the Emergency Department.

As part of this process, I reviewed the clinical timeline of the patient's journey from arrival to the eventual diagnosis and initiation of treatment. This allowed me to map out the sequence of events and identify exactly where the clinical care fell below the expected standard.
draft_fields.root_causes: The primary root cause was the delayed recognition of sepsis due to critical gaps in the handover process between shifts, which led to a loss of continuity of care and a failure to appreciate the patient's deteriorating clinical trajectory.
draft_fields.contributing_factors: Contributing factors included high department volume, incomplete documentation of the initial physiological parameters, and a lack of a structured, mandatory tool to prompt handover of deteriorating patients in majors.
draft_fields.learning_points:
I learned the critical importance of handover practices, especially during high-activity shifts in majors. 

I have learned to use an explicit, objective trigger for senior clinical review when a patient's physiological parameters do not improve as expected or when there is any ambiguity during transition of care.
draft_fields.further_action: I will share the findings of this serious incident review with the department team to raise awareness about handover safety. I will consistently apply explicit senior review triggers in my own practice and support junior colleagues in recognising these triggers early.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

draft_fields.reflective_notes: The course refreshed my trauma team approach, the primary survey, and my communication with the team leader. I reflected on the importance of maintaining a structured approach during high-pressure trauma resuscitations.
draft_fields.lessons_learned: I will apply the structured handover in resus to clear and effective communication during trauma team handovers.

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. My consultant assessed me during a three-hour evening ED shift. I managed flow in majors, supported juniors, reviewed two unwell patients and reflected on maintaining situational awareness under pressure.

recommended_forms.rationale: A consultant formally observed the trainee's non-technical skills, flow management, and junior support over a three-hour shift period, satisfying the ESLE/ESLE_ASSESS criteria.
The trainee explicitly reflects on maintaining situational awareness under pressure during this high-intensity shift.
The trainee actively managed majors flow and supported juniors, representing a clear clinical leadership and departmental coordination role.

draft_fields.reflection:
During this three-hour evening shift, I acted as the shift lead to manage flow in the majors area, support the junior team, and directly review two unwell patients. Balancing the operational oversight of the department with the clinical demands of critically ill patients required constant prioritisation. 

Maintaining overall situational awareness is challenging when drawn into individual high-acuity cases. I focused on delegating tasks to competent juniors and I was accessible for advice, which helped keep the department moving safely. 

In future shifts, I will continue to practice this balance, I do not get too task-focused on single patients when holding the flow portfolio. I will actively prompt my juniors for updates to maintain an accurate cognitive map of the department.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. Senior review prompted a PE workup. I learned to pause when the story does not fit and to document my differential more explicitly.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a patient with acute breathlessness, requiring clinical reasoning, differential diagnosis formulation (COPD vs. PE), and retrospective discussion with a senior clinician.
The trainee explicitly reflects on cognitive bias (anchoring on COPD) and describes specific learning points regarding clinical practice and documentation changes.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflecting on anchoring bias in a breathless patient
draft_fields.reflection:
I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. I focused on this respiratory history during my initial assessment and started standard treatment.

During a senior review, the consultant prompted me to consider a PE workup. This challenged my initial diagnosis and led to a broader investigation.
draft_fields.replay_differently: I would perform a deliberate re-evaluation pause to challenge my working diagnosis before finalizing my management plan.
draft_fields.why: I anchored on the patient's known history of COPD, which closed my mind to alternative causes of breathlessness.
draft_fields.different_outcome: The clinical outcome was positive because of the timely senior review, but escalating with a broader documented differential would have led to an earlier PE workup.
draft_fields.focussing_on: I am focussing on incorporating a structured diagnostic pause into my assessment of patients with acute-on-chronic presentations.
draft_fields.learned: I learned to pause when the clinical story does not fit perfectly and to document my differential diagnosis more explicitly.
key_capabilities: SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. A consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries. They fed back that my explanations were clear but I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first.

recommended_forms.rationale: Teaching was observed for feedback on the trainee's teaching skill.
The same activity can also be recorded as teaching delivered.

draft_fields.reflection:
I delivered a teaching session to an F2 doctor on how to assess ankle injuries, which was observed by a consultant.

The consultant fed back that my explanations were clear, but advised that I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first before delivering the content.

In future teaching sessions, I will start with a brief question-and-answer discussion to establish the learner's current level of understanding. This will allow me to tailor my teaching more effectively to their specific needs.

I completed an audit of documentation of capacity assessments in ED mental health presentations. I reviewed 30 notes, presented results and proposed a documentation prompt.

recommended_forms.rationale: The user explicitly asked for Audit Assessment Tool.

draft_fields.reflection:
I completed an audit of the documentation of capacity assessments in ED mental health presentations, reviewing 30 patient notes.

The audit demonstrated varying standards in how clearly capacity assessments are recorded. To address this gap and improve patient safety, I presented the results to the department and proposed a documentation prompt to guide clinicians during these assessments.

This project of documentation in complex mental health presentations. I learned how targeted clinical governance and simple system-level prompts can directly support staff in meeting legal and professional standards.
